inputs.story: The California condor is one of the world's largest and most endangered birds. Several conservation programs have been raising and releasing condors into the wild in an effort to boost their numbers. Using only the clues that follow, determine the age and wingspan of each of the most recently released condors, and determine the location in which each was set free. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird.
2. The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old.
3. Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches.
4. The four condors are the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin and the condor with a wingspan of 106 inches.
5. The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird.
inputs.constants:
wingspans: 102 inches; 106 inches; 110 inches; 114 inches.
names: Merlin; Pepper; Spike; Sunshine.
ages: 4; 5; 8; 9.
inputs.solution:
Wingspans	Names	Ages
102 inches	Sunshine	9
106 inches	Spike	4
110 inches	Merlin	8
114 inches	Pepper	5

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
The California condor is one of the world's largest and most endangered birds. Several conservation programs have been raising and releasing condors into the wild in an effort to boost their numbers. Using only the clues that follow, determine the age and wingspan of each of the most recently released condors, and determine the location in which each was set free. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird.
2. The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old.
3. Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches.
4. The four condors are the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin and the condor with a wingspan of 106 inches.
5. The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird.

Constants:
wingspans: 102 inches; 106 inches; 110 inches; 114 inches.
names: Merlin; Pepper; Spike; Sunshine.
ages: 4; 5; 8; 9.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program in Clingo format that solves a specific logic grid puzzle. The program must follow the provided examples and rules strictly.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Categories**:
     - Wingspans: 102, 106, 110, 114 (inches) -> constants: `102`, `106`, `110`, `114`
     - Names: Merlin, Pepper, Spike, Sunshine -> constants: `"Merlin"`, `"Pepper"`, `"Spike"`, `"Sunshine"`
     - Ages: 4, 5, 8, 9 -> constants: `4`, `5`, `8`, `9`
   - **Clues**:
     1. Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird.
     2. The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old.
     3. Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches.
     4. The four condors are the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin and the condor with a wingspan of 106 inches. (Implies these four are distinct entities, but since they are the only condors, it actually means: The 9yo, Pepper, Merlin, and the 106" condor are all different. Wait, it says "The four condors are...", which in logic puzzles usually means these four descriptions refer to the four distinct condors in the puzzle. So they must all be distinct from each other.)
     5. The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird.

3.  **Map Categories to Constants**:
   - `wing(102; 106; 110; 114).`
   - `name("Merlin"; "Pepper"; "Spike"; "Sunshine").`
   - `age(4; 5; 8; 9).`

4.  **Design Predicates & Search Space**:
   - We need to assign exactly one wing, one name, and one age to each condor.
   - Let's anchor on `name`. Predicate: `condor(N, W, A).` (Name, Wingspan, Age)
   - Search space: `{condor(N, W, A) : wing(W), age(A)} = 1 :- name(N).`
   - Uniqueness: `{N1=N2; W1=W2; A1=A2} = 0 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), (N1,W1,A1) != (N2,W2,A2).`

5.  **Encode Clues**:
   - *Clue 1*: Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird.
     - `102` or `age(5)` applies to `name("Sunshine")`.
     - ASP: `{W=102; A=5} = 1 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Sunshine".`
   - *Clue 2*: The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old.
     - If `W=114`, then `A=5`. Also implies `W=114` and `A=5` are the same condor.
     - ASP: `A=5 :- condor(N, W, A), W=114.`
     - Also, since it's a 1-to-1 mapping, we can just state the relationship. `A=5 :- W=114.` is fine.
     - Wait, does it imply exactly one condor has 114 wingspan and it's 5yo? Yes.
     - Let's write: `A=5 :- condor(N, W, A), W=114.`
   - *Clue 3*: Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches.
     - `W=114 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Pepper".`
     - Combined with Clue 2: Pepper is 5 years old. (We can just encode Clue 3 directly).
   - *Clue 4*: The four condors are the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin and the condor with a wingspan of 106 inches.
     - This means these four descriptions refer to four distinct condors.
     - So: `age(9)`, `name("Pepper")`, `name("Merlin")`, `wing(106)` are all different.
     - We need pairwise inequalities between the condors having these attributes.
     - Let `C1` be condor with `A=9`, `C2` with `N="Pepper"`, `C3` with `N="Merlin"`, `C4` with `W=106`.
     - Actually, since we anchor on names, we can express this as:
       - `condor("Pepper", _, _)` is different from `condor(_, 106, _)` (unless Pepper has 106, but they are different condors, so Pepper != condor(106))
       - `condor("Merlin", _, _)` is different from `condor(_, 106, _)`
       - `condor("Merlin", _, _)` is different from `condor(_, _, 9)`
       - `condor("Pepper", _, _)` is different from `condor(_, _, 9)`
     - Wait, the clue says "The four condors are...". This means these four attributes belong to four different condors. So:
       - `N1 != N2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, N2="Pepper".` (9yo != Pepper)
       - `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), A1=9, N3="Merlin".` (9yo != Merlin)
       - `N1 != N2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), N1="Pepper", W2=106.` (Pepper != 106")
       - `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.` (Merlin != 106")
       - `A1 != A3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Pepper", A3=9.` (Pepper != 9yo)
       - `W1 != W3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.` (Merlin != 106")
     - Actually, a simpler way for "A, B, C, D are all different" in this grid format is to ensure no condor has more than one of these attributes. Since each condor has exactly one name, one age, one wing, we can just say:
       - `A1 != A2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, N2="Pepper".`
       - `A1 != A3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), A1=9, N3="Merlin".`
       - `A1 != A2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), N2="Pepper", W1=106.` (Wait, this compares age of 9yo to age of 106" condor? No, we want to ensure the condor with age 9 is not the condor with wing 106. So `A1 != A2` works if we bind them to the same condor? No.
     - Let's stick to the standard pattern from Example 2:
       `C1 != C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), G1=3, S2=6.`
       Here, we can do:
       `N1 != N2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, N2="Pepper".`
       `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), A1=9, N3="Merlin".`
       `N1 != N2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), N1="Pepper", W2=106.`
       `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.`
       `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Pepper", A3=9.`
       `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.` (Already covered)
       Wait, we need to ensure all four are distinct. The four descriptors are: 9yo, Pepper, Merlin, 106".
       So we need inequalities between: (9yo vs Pepper), (9yo vs Merlin), (9yo vs 106"), (Pepper vs Merlin), (Pepper vs 106"), (Merlin vs 106").
       Since we anchor on names, we can express these as:
       `A1 != A2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, N2="Pepper".` (9yo != Pepper)
       `A1 != A3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), A1=9, N3="Merlin".` (9yo != Merlin)
       `N1 != N2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), N1="Pepper", W2=106.` (Pepper != 106")
       `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.` (Merlin != 106")
       `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Pepper", A3=9.` (Pepper != 9yo)
       `W1 != W3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.` (Merlin != 106") -> Wait, comparing wingspans of Pepper and 106" condor doesn't make sense if we anchor on names. Actually, `N1 != N2` is enough to say they are different condors. If we want to say "Pepper is not the 106" condor", we can say `W1 != 106` for `N1="Pepper"`.
       Let's simplify: The clue just lists four distinct condors. We can enforce that no condor satisfies more than one of these properties.
       - `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Pepper".` (Pepper is not 9yo)
       - `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Merlin".` (Merlin is not 9yo)
       - `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), W=106.` (106" is not 9yo)
       - `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Pepper".` (Pepper is not 106")
       - `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Merlin".` (Merlin is not 106")
       - `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), A=9.` (9yo is not 106")
       This covers all pairwise distinctness constraints cleanly.
   - *Clue 5*: The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird.
     - `W1 = A2 - 4 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, A2=4.`
     - Check: 9yo wingspan = 4yo wingspan - 4. So if 4yo has 110, 9yo has 106. If 4yo has 106, 9yo has 102. If 4yo has 102, 9yo has 98 (not in list). If 4yo has 114, 9yo has 110.
     - Wait, wingspans are 102, 106, 110, 114. Differences of 4: (106,110) and (110,114). So 9yo could be 106 or 110.
     - ASP: `W1 = W2 - 4 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, A2=4.`

6.  **Review & Refine**:
   Let's assemble the ASP program step-by-step and verify against rules.

   Constants:
   ```asp
   wing(102; 106; 110; 114).
   name("Merlin"; "Pepper"; "Spike"; "Sunshine").
   age(4; 5; 8; 9).
   ```

   Search space:
   ```asp
   {condor(N, W, A) : wing(W), age(A)} = 1 :- name(N).
   ```

   Uniqueness:
   ```asp
   {N1=N2; W1=W2; A1=A2} = 0 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), (N1,W1,A1) != (N2,W2,A2).
   ```

   Clues:
   1. Sunshine is either 102" or 5yo.
      `{W=102; A=5} = 1 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Sunshine".`
   2. 114" is 5yo.
      `A=5 :- condor(N, W, A), W=114.`
   3. Pepper has 114".
      `W=114 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Pepper".`
      (Note: Clue 2 & 3 imply Pepper is 5yo. This is consistent.)
   4. Four condors are 9yo, Pepper, Merlin, 106". All distinct.
      Using the "no condor satisfies more than one property" approach:
      `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Pepper".`
      `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Merlin".`
      `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), W=106.`
      `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Pepper".`
      `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Merlin".`
      `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), A=9.`
      This correctly enforces that Pepper, Merlin, the 9yo, and the 106" bird are all different condors.
   5. 9yo wingspan is 4" shorter than 4yo wingspan.
      `W1 = W2 - 4 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, A2=4.`

   Let's double check Clue 4 encoding. The pattern from Example 2 uses `C1!=C2 :- ...`. Since we anchor on names, using `N1 != N2` or restricting attributes on a single name is fine. The "no condor satisfies more than one property" is logically equivalent and often cleaner. I'll stick with it, but I'll format it similarly to the examples if possible. Actually, the example uses:
   `C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), G1=3, S2=6.`
   I can do:
   `N1 != N2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, N2="Pepper".`
   `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), A1=9, N3="Merlin".`
   `N1 != N2 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), N1="Pepper", W2=106.`
   `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.`
   `N1 != N3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Pepper", A3=9.`
   `W1 != W3 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N3, W3, A3), N1="Merlin", W3=106.` -> Wait, comparing wingspans of different condors doesn't enforce they are different condors. I'll stick to the attribute restriction method or use `N1 != N2` style.
   Actually, the simplest is:
   `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Pepper".`
   `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Merlin".`
   `A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), W=106.`
   `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Pepper".`
   `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Merlin".`
   `W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), A=9.`
   This is perfectly valid and unambiguous.

   Let's verify Clue 1: "Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird."
   `{W=102; A=5} = 1 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Sunshine".` -> Correct.

   Clue 2: "The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old."
   `A=5 :- condor(N, W, A), W=114.` -> Correct.

   Clue 3: "Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches."
   `W=114 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Pepper".` -> Correct.

   Clue 5: "The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird."
   `W1 = W2 - 4 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, A2=4.` -> Correct.

   Check uniqueness rule format:
   `{N1=N2; W1=W2; A1=A2}=0 :- condor(N1,W1,A1), condor(N2,W2,A2), (N1,W1,A1)!=(N2,W2,A2).` -> Matches example.

   Check show directive: `#show condor/3.`

   Let's mentally solve to ensure it's consistent and has a unique solution.
   - Pepper: W=114 (Clue 3) -> A=5 (Clue 2). So Pepper: 114, 5.
   - 9yo vs 4yo wingspan: W_9 = W_4 - 4.
     Possible pairs (W_9, W_4): (102, 106) or (106, 110) or (110, 114).
     But 114 is taken by Pepper (age 5). So (110, 114) is out.
     Remaining: (102, 106) or (106, 110).
   - Clue 4 says 9yo, Pepper, Merlin, 106" are all different.
     So 9yo != 106". Thus (106, 110) is out because W_9 would be 106.
     So W_9 must be 102, and W_4 must be 106.
     So 9yo: W=102. 4yo: W=106.
   - We have:
     Pepper: W=114, A=5
     9yo: W=102, A=9

asp_generation.response:
```asp
% Constants
wing(102; 106; 110; 114).
name("Merlin"; "Pepper"; "Spike"; "Sunshine").
age(4; 5; 8; 9).

% Search space: for each condor name N, assign exactly one wingspan W and age A.
{condor(N, W, A) : wing(W), age(A)} = 1 :- name(N).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{N1=N2; W1=W2; A1=A2} = 0 :- condor(N1,W1,A1), condor(N2,W2,A2), (N1,W1,A1)!=(N2,W2,A2).

% 1. Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird.
{W=102; A=5} = 1 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Sunshine".

% 2. The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old.
A=5 :- condor(N, W, A), W=114.

% 3. Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches.
W=114 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Pepper".

% 4. The four condors are the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin and the condor with a wingspan of 106 inches.
A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Pepper".
A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Merlin".
A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), W=106.
W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Pepper".
W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Merlin".
W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), A=9.

% 5. The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird.
W1 = W2 - 4 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, A2=4.

#show condor/3.
```

% Constants
wing(102; 106; 110; 114).
name("Merlin"; "Pepper"; "Spike"; "Sunshine").
age(4; 5; 8; 9).

% Search space: for each condor name N, assign exactly one wingspan W and age A.
{condor(N, W, A) : wing(W), age(A)} = 1 :- name(N).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{N1=N2; W1=W2; A1=A2} = 0 :- condor(N1,W1,A1), condor(N2,W2,A2), (N1,W1,A1)!=(N2,W2,A2).

% 1. Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird.
{W=102; A=5} = 1 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Sunshine".

% 2. The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old.
A=5 :- condor(N, W, A), W=114.

% 3. Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches.
W=114 :- condor(N, W, A), N="Pepper".

% 4. The four condors are the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin and the condor with a wingspan of 106 inches.
A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Pepper".
A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), N="Merlin".
A1 != 9 :- condor(N, W, A1), W=106.
W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Pepper".
W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), N="Merlin".
W1 != 106 :- condor(N, W1, A), A=9.

% 5. The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird.
W1 = W2 - 4 :- condor(N1, W1, A1), condor(N2, W2, A2), A1=9, A2=4.

#show condor/3.
